data.title: Synchrony Financial 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Synchrony Financial in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: T-Mobile: Back In Time First, And Then A Look Forward
data.description:
By Levi at Elliott Wave Trader; Produced with Avi Gilburt

What Truly Moves Markets?

We follow the fundamentals with Lyn Alden. Fundamentals are important. However, they typically will frame a thesis for a 3 to 5 year time period. As well, we have observed that fundamentals rarely lead the stock price on a chart.

We’re going to go back in time, as it were, for a real-life case study in (NASDAQ:TMUS) as it unfolded over the past year. Along the way we had several updates as the stock progressed inside the anticipated structure. Then, we’ll take a look forward to project what is most likely for the chart. And, please note that this study is not based purely on the fundamentals of the stock. Why not?

Well, if it isn't fundamentals that lead, what is the driving force of important turning points in the markets? Sentiment. Simply put, fear and greed that battle against one another until the force of one overpowers the other.

Sellers will exhaust themselves at bottoms and buyers famously flame out at tops. All have observed fundamentals that get strenuously stretched to what appear to be absurd valuations to both extremes. Markets seem to be what we deem 'irrational' at these points. This is because markets are not logical, thinking entities. They are fluid, dynamic and non-linear in nature. Markets are sentiment in action before our very eyes.

Note this brief excerpt from an article that Avi Gilburt wrote regarding what truly moves markets. The piece was entitled, 'This Analysis Will Change The Way You Invest Forever'. In Part-2 (you can read it here) of the six-part series, Avi shared this point regarding Ralph Nelson Elliott's market research and findings:

In 1941, Elliott stated, regarding the financial markets, that "[t]hese [Fibonacci] ratios and series have been controlling and limited the extent and duration of price trends, irrespective of wars, politics, production indices, the supply of money, general purchasing power, and other generally accepted methods of determining stock values." As you can see, the more research conducted into this subject, the more support we find to Elliott's theories set out almost 100 years ago.

Every day, we take this theory and put it into practice for our members. This is illustrated across all markets and sectors. And, although the theory is just that, we believe that we have proven it’s dynamic utility and adaptability literally scores of times each week. Here’s just one way that we use it in a stock like TMUS.

Wave Setups Point To A High-Probability Scenario for TMUS

We have a regular feature that we simply refer to as "Wave Setups". These are charts that Zac Mannes and Garrett Patten will post along with specific targets and also invalidation points for each setup. Let’s track their progress starting back in August of 2023 when (TMUS) was featured as a bullish scenario with clear parameters for targets, support, resistance and invalidation of the setup.

From here, I would like to share just some of the key updates that were provided to members along the way. There were many more than what is in this portion of this piece, but I think you get the general idea of how the feature works.

Now, note in this latest update from a week ago that Zac is turning more cautious. Why is this? Bigger picture targets are being reached. What’s more, the highest probability portion of this bullish move is likely in place. Can the stock trend higher? Of course it can. But since we view the markets through a probabilistic lens, as Zac mentions, the risk vs reward skew is not favorable at this time.

So, What’s Likely Next For TMUS?

Well, I’m glad you asked. Let’s have a look at what the structure of price on the chart suggests as the probable path.

You can readily see from both of our lead analysts that we anticipate price to consolidate and pull back in a fourth wave. Since these can be highly variable in their structure, many may choose to stand aside as this issue works off the steam from the last bullish run. Third waves are typically the strongest of the waves in a 5 wave structure. So, yes, this can yet extend higher but we are turning cautious at this time.

It would seem that this anticipated consolidation could take several months and may lead to a correction to the $161 - $173 level. We will track the subwave structure of this decline once it begins to materialize in the larger degree. For now, it is expected to form an a-b-c, 3 wave structure for the fourth wave as you see on Zac and Garrett’s charts.

How Can We Use This Information?

This really depends on an individual’s time frame, risk tolerance, portfolio goals, etc. However, we can outline guidelines and then each person can protect, hedge, take profits, set stop losses and the like according to their own plan.

In this current case, should TMUS find a swing high here in the $218 area then the potential drawdown could be hedged with options or another similar strategy.

Conclusion

There are many ways to analyze and track stocks and the market they form. Some are more consistent than others. For us, this method has proved the most reliable and keeps us on the right side of the trade much more often than not. Nothing is perfect in this world, but for those looking to open their eyes to a new universe of trading and investing, why not consider studying this further? It may just be one of the most illuminating projects you undertake.

(Housekeeping Matters)

If you would like notifications as to when our new articles are published, please hit the button at the bottom of the page to "Follow" us.

data.title: Bank of America Q3: Why I Sold Shares After Earnings
data.description:
Yesterday, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) released its fiscal third quarter earnings. The release beat expectations on revenue as well as on EPS. Going into the release, expectations were running high, as competitor JPMorgan Chase (JPM) had days prior put out earnings that handily beat expectations and ignited a rally in bank shares. It would have been very easy for Bank of America to disappoint following JPMorgan’s strong performance. Thankfully, the bank’s actual results pleased the markets and delivered a good price to sell at.

If you’ve followed my recent coverage of Bank of America stock, then you’ll know that I’ve been getting less bullish on it over time. It’s not that I think the company itself has deteriorated, or anything, but rather that the stock price has gone up too much. At this point, Bank of America is trading at 14.76 times earnings and 1.22 times book value. With $115 billion in unrealized losses, $296 billion in equity and 7.9 billion shares outstanding, the “adjusted” book value per share is 22.91, and the adjusted price/book is more like 1.83.

The above multiples are actually fairly high by bank standards. Although they are lower than those of the S&P 500 at the moment, banks usually trade at discounts because of heavy use of leverage and various regulations that limit how much of their earnings and assets can be distributed to shareholders.

BAC’s multiples are definitely higher than the norm for banking as a whole. For example, the 14.76 P/E ratio is higher than the 8.4-13.5 times earnings range that banks normally trade at. So, there is a case to be made that BAC is overvalued on a sector-relative basis. Seeking Alpha Quant seems to agree, giving BAC a merely-average ‘C’ on valuation.

Apart from the valuation concerns, there are concerns with Bank of America’s just-released earnings, too. Although the release was technically a beat and did trigger a minor rally in BAC shares, there were reasons to find the ‘absolute’ results underwhelming. The results showed a 10% decline in earnings, a tiny 1.3% jump in revenue, and very tepid growth in most segments. The increase in investment banking fees and the 1.8% q/q decline in share count were commendable, but the top and bottom line results were underwhelming.

When I last covered Bank of America, I rated it a hold because it was up dramatically after an earnings release I didn’t see as all that great. Now we have the stock again up after a just ‘so-so’ earnings release and trading above the historical norm for bank stocks as a whole. Seeing all this, I trimmed my position by 33% after the earnings release came out. In this article I will share the reasons why I trimmed my Bank of America position after earnings.

Earnings Recap

To kick things off, I'll share a summary of Bank of America’s third quarter earnings release, particularly the parts of it that led to my decision to sell BAC shares.

For the third quarter, Bank of America reported:

  • Net revenue of $25.3 billion, up 1.3% (a beat).

  • $0.81 in diluted EPS, down 10% (also a beat).

  • $14 billion in net interest income (NII), down 3% sequentially and up 2% y/y.

  • $1.5 billion in provisions for credit losses (PCLs), basically unchanged.

  • $114.7 billion in unrealized losses on debt securities.

  • $1.4 billion in investment banking fees, up 18%.

There were definite positives in this picture, but there were concerns as well. The unrealized loss remained among the largest of BAC’s peers who reported for the period. The high growth in investment banking looked nice, but the growth wasn’t anywhere near what JPMorgan did in the same period (31%). Finally, the persistently large unrealized loss was not a positive. Although I never thought that the large unrealized loss was an existential threat to BAC, it does make about a third of the company’s reported book value a mirage. So I consider the persistently large unrealized loss on BAC’s balance sheet a bad thing, one that must be compensated for.

Future Prospects

Having looked at Bank of America’s most recent quarterly results, we can now turn to the company’s future prospects. A good starting point here is the company’s guidance from its most recent earnings call.

In their most recent earnings call, BAC’s management team offered the following forward-looking statements:

  • Continued NII growth in Q4 (no specific figure).

  • A positive earnings impact from the BSBY acquisition.

  • Continued deposit growth.

The bank did not give specific numerical guidance for the fourth quarter but its management team did sound vaguely enthusiastic and upbeat on the call. Overall, I would expect a solidly profitable but growth-less Q4 based on what Bank of America’s management said on the call.

We can also turn to macro trends to help gauge what will happen with BAC in Q4.

We know that:

  1. The Fed is cutting interest rates, which should cut into the NII margin somewhat.

  2. Dealmaking at investment banks is up, and this can be expected to continue as lower rates make investors more eager to invest. So we’d expect the high growth in IB fees to continue.

  3. The wealth management business should also see increased client activity due to the aforementioned factors.

Given the mixed signals above, I’d expect Bank of America to put out earnings next quarter that are unchanged year-over-year. This is pretty close to what management hinted at on the call, so I feel the signals I’m operating off here are material.

Valuation

Now we can turn to valuing Bank of America shares in light of the recent earnings release and call.

First, here are some select multiples courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant:

Some of these multiples appear appealing, especially when you compare them to the S&P 500. However, remember that banks are usually cheaper than the markets as a whole due to leverage and liquidity risks and various restraining regulations. Also, BAC’s price/book multiple increases to 1.83 when you adjust it for the unrealized loss on held-to-maturity securities. With these considerations in mind, BAC stock is not as cheap as it looks.

Since I’ve estimated 0% earnings growth for BAC for the near term, I can discount its cash flows based on that assumption.

Bank of America’s normalized EPS is $2.29. The 10-year treasury yield is 4%. I sometimes use risk premia as high as 5% when valuing stocks, but since I have many factors from both the earnings call and the economy to support my “0% forward growth for BAC” thesis, I’ll just use 1% here. So we’re discounting $2.29 at 5%. That produces a $45.8 fair value estimate–some upside, but not a whole lot.

Given that I acquired most of my Bank of America shares below $30, and that the stock is much closer to being fully valued today than it was when I bought it, I made the decision to trim my BAC at $42. At $45, I would exit the position.

The Bottom Line

Bank of America is doing reasonably well this year, but is close to being fully valued. By my estimates it’s worth somewhere between $45 and $46. I consider the stock a hold now and I’d consider it a sell at $45-$46. So, I’m happy to have trimmed my position.

data.title: Palantir: The New AI Bubble Favorite At 135x Earnings (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have not been bullish on Palantir Technologies Inc. (NYSE:PLTR) since the low $20s were reached about a year ago for a share price. My last article discussing valuation issues was published in June here. Fortunately for shareholders, bubbles grow in size until they pop.

Never mind common sense, the valuation and quote have continued to balloon on AI-related excitement. In fact, I now place this stock above NVIDIA (NVDA) for holding the greatest overvaluation vs. likely operating outcomes during 2025-26.

If you will, Palantir may have morphed into the "most" overhyped of all AI names since the summer in the large-cap Big Tech area of the U.S. stock market at $42 per share. As a consequence, I am downgrading my 12-month view to Strong Sell.

The Business

While NVIDIA makes the basic high-speed computer chips for artificial intelligence datacenters, Palantir is a leading software and database designer for businesses and governments. The bullish argument is massive EPS growth is around the corner on higher profit margins and better returns on equity/assets than even NVIDIA. I am not completely sold on the idea. However, speculative fever what it is, has taken hold and decided PLTR is a must-own for growth portfolios.

For sure, growth rates have been above average vs. other businesses in America. Yet, in my humble opinion, 20% to 30% annual income increases are not the "amazing" growth story to deserve a valuation of better than 135x trailing after-tax cash earnings (250x GAAP results).

The Q2 Earnings Presentation highlighted lots of good news in terms of new customer additions, rising sales, and much improved income generation. I don't have anything bearish to say about operations or management. The problem is what investors are paying upfront to own a piece of the business.

Bubble Valuation Story

In the olden days before the internet took off during the early 1990s, growth-stock valuations were typically considered high at 10x "trailing" sales and 50x EPS. However, the AI darlings of today (huge corporations, mind you), are priced well outside of this usual box. Believe it or not, the Palantir share price to sales ratio on the latest 12-month operating total is 40x, while the standard valuation comparison on GAAP earnings is a whopping 250x!

NVIDIA's setup is much cheaper on these traditional yardsticks of equity worth. A valuation of 34x trailing sales and 62x earnings is still crazy high, especially for a company with a $3 trillion market capitalization. However, it makes stronger mathematical sense than Palantir's lower growth-rate background.

Other eye-popping valuation datasets for Palantir include an enterprise valuation (equity + debt - cash) on "forward" sales of 33x and EBITDA of 93x.

In fact, NVIDIA is again valued far cheaper using enterprise valuation ratio analysis, sitting at an EV to forward revenue number of 27x and EBITDA of 41x.

Really, for me, the problem lies in the growth forecast for Palantir. It is excellent, but not overly stupendous. If sales were expanding 30%+ annually, and earnings closer to 50% per annum, you could make a reasonable case for the current valuation stats. But, Wall Street analysts are projecting closer to 20% sales growth yearly, alongside a similar EPS rate of expansion after 2024 is complete.

This is what forward 1-year guidance looks like for the two AI winners, using a P/E ratio measurement. Given current 2025 estimates, Palantir is valued at 3x the equivalent metric for NVIDIA (98x vs. 33x)!

Then, when we cross Palantir's greater valuation setup with a slower expected growth rate for earnings, forward PEG Ratio comparisons (price to earnings divided by earnings growth rate) tell investors exactly what they need to know. The bottom line is, new buyers are paying TWICE the growth-adjusted valuation for Palantir as NVIDIA (which itself is a Sell idea in my research). This is a far cry from the nearly equal setup in June.

Don't trust what I am saying? Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system gives PLTR shares an "F" for a Valuation Grade. At this stage of nuttiness, I would give it an "F-" if possible.

Palantir's valuation stats are screaming this stock's price future is too good to be true. We're talking about "overvaluation" datapoints in the 100s to 1,000s of a percent higher vs. regular computer software and SaaS businesses.

Final Thoughts

With $4 billion in cash and no debt, you would think the company has a terrific backstop for shareholder worth on the balance sheet. Yes and No. Against $97 billion in equity market capitalization, you are only getting 5 cents on the dollar in equivalent cash holdings.

In addition, roughly $700 million in trailing free cash flow generation ($800 million to $1 billion on forward management guidance) annually sounds like something to cheer. Not so fast. It only creates a free cash flow yield of 0.7% for owners! Why not just invest in T-Bills yielding 4.5% annually, with zero risk to your upfront investment? At a theoretical 20% long-term EPS growth rate, it will take a full decade (10 years) just to match the risk-free cash yields available today! At 25%, it would take eight years. And with no dividend yield from Palantir, you may lose out on 40% to 45% (with compounding) in cash dividend gains owning Treasuries as an alternative.

However you slice it, the free cash flow "goal" of owning any business looks closer to a total failure in mathematical terms for Palantir shareholders. One of my valuation imperatives is to own a business with high free cash flow yield, definitely better than prevailing cash investment yields.

Insider/management sales have also been increasing in 2024, from the likes of Co-Founder Peter Thiel and CEO/Founder Alex Karp. Around $1.5 billion in net value has been sold over the past 12 months.

Like other Big Tech names in October 2024, those committing capital to PLTR today are counting on the Greater Fool Theory of new investors coming to the rescue to bid the quote ever higher, with little underlying value supporting your buy decision at $42. You are counting on the boom in AI equities growing even larger. You are betting valuations no longer matter for investment decision making, where the fear of missing out (FOMO) on stock appreciation overrides all rational analysis. It's the same thought process, part and parcel of every bubble situation.

What could save the stock quote from falling hard soon? I would say growth rates have to pick up for sales and earnings. Huge expansion at NVIDIA has helped the stock quote to double and triple, despite a super-extended valuation a year ago. If Palantir growth rates at the operating business expand from the 20% to 30% YoY range above 40%+ in coming quarters, I can see the share price trading between $35 and $50 in 2025.

How can Palantir achieve stronger growth rates? Management understands it must expand its customer base from mostly government entities to big business to accelerate growth. Palantir's AI Bootcamps are an interesting sales angle in this regard. Thousands of companies have taken a look at what Palantir's database experience and software code can do to help efficiency and profit levels. So, closely watch the level of sales outside of U.S. government accounts (representing about 50% of sales), including the number of new clients/customers brought to the table in the Q3 earnings release due November 4th, 2024.

The flip side of this equation is growth rates could just as easily stall in a recession scenario. What if future growth rates are being overstated? What if Palantir actually only grows 15% a year? With today's massive overvaluation, there would be hell to pay. A stock implosion to $20 or even $15 would be quite easy, as investors rerate the stock with a more typical and sustainable technology valuation.

So, you have a risk-reward balance of potentially gaining +15% to +20% for an investment return, offset by -50% to -65% of theoretical downside. My conclusion: outside of a "Goldilocks" scenario for both the economy and AI leaders (including a strengthening economy with sliding inflation/interest rates), I am projecting Palantir will be an oversized investment loser over the next 12 months.

I am moving my PLTR rating from Sell to Strong Sell. When the AI bust appears (and it will eventually), I believe Palantir will be a leader to the downside. If price trades back to $20, we can reevaluate the investment picture and outlook.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Unlocking Massive 13% Yields By Busting Market Myths: Annaly Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

Starting in 2003, MythBusters began its storied run on television. It aimed to address the various myths that had accumulated in popular culture and determine whether there was any truth in them. Depending on the source, they addressed over 800 to 1000 myths in their 14-year run — quite a monumental total.

When it comes to the market, investors have many time-honored myths. Usually, these start off as complete misunderstandings that, through repetition, become erroneously accepted as fact. As retail investors gained faster and easier access to the market, various myths became even more prevalent.

No sector seems to be ripe territory for myths than the high-yield sector. If we dig deeper into this sector, we can see that the mortgage real estate investment trust, or mREIT, is filled with massive misunderstandings. Many will tell you that the sector is meant only for traders, those who are willing to buy and sell rapidly to take advantage of the price movements. Others will tell you that it is ripe with risk, while misunderstanding the very product that these companies hold.

Today I want to zero in on some myths in this sector, address them using a perfect example, and show you how you can collect double-digit yields just by understanding what other people choose to accept blindly — even though they're myths.

Let's dive in!

Myth Busting Time

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY), yielding 13.2%, is a mortgage REIT that invests in agency MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities). Mortgage REITs are perhaps the most hated segment of the market. This is somewhat understandable, as mortgage REITs of various types have seen substantial headwinds recently. As a result, a large majority of them have seen their prices decline. Interest rates have played a significant role in that because with high interest rates the price of substantially all debt investments declined considerably. What do mortgage REITs invest in? By definition, they invest in debt. If they invested in something apart from debt, they wouldn't be known as a mortgage REIT. What has happened to the prices of mortgages?

A higher yield means lower prices.

So, we get the steady drumbeat of bearish articles, “The returns over the past X years are horrible!!!”

Of course. Look at the chart. Higher yield means lower prices. There is no mystery why yields are high, and it has nothing to do with the market predicting some massive wave of foreclosures. We're coming off the most aggressive rate hiking cycle in modern times. That hiking caused plenty of downsides for debt investments of all types:

Note the chart above includes junk bonds, investment-grade bonds, bank loans, residential mortgages, and US Treasuries. What do they all have in common? They are all down in price. Each one is down in price from high credit risk (junk bonds) to no credit risk (US Treasuries) — even bank loans which are floating rate.

Today, I want to focus on NLY because its price is pretty close to all-time lows. In fact, NLY has underperformed so much recently that the S&P 500 (SP500) actually managed to catch up, and for the first time since 2001, the S&P 500 has a higher total return than NLY.

It says something about an investment that it has to be trading within spitting distance of all-time low prices for the S&P 500 to have approximately equal total return. Ultimately, that is the power of dividends.

Why has this historical outperformer been underperforming recently? As we've explained in numerous other articles, it boils down to an inverted yield curve combined with Agency MBS trading at all-time lows – two things that were directly caused by the Federal Reserve's hiking cycle.

Yet, even as these headwinds have dissipated, I have seen numerous statements trying to explain why this time is different and why NLY isn't going to recover. Today, I want to address two of these myths.

Myth #1: Mortgage REITs have credit risk. If homeowners default on their mortgages, that is negative for NLY.

This is one of the sillier arguments that I've seen some people making. Agency mortgages do not have credit risk. If a borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgages at par value. As a result, a borrower defaulting is indistinguishable from a borrower repaying the loan. For NLY, a default is identical to a prepayment, and defaults are included in their reported and projected prepayment rates.

Some mortgage REITs do carry credit risk if they invest in non-agency MBS or commercial mortgages. NLY does carry some exposure to credit risk, which accounts for about 20% of its equity: Source.

However, since its agency portfolio is leveraged at 10x debt/equity, what happens in the agency portion of the portfolio dominates the rest of the portfolio. While it accounts for 58% of the equity, agency MBS accounts for 88% of the assets.

Myth #2: Lower interest rates will be bad for NLY because prepayments will accelerate.

Those who followed NLY from 2012-2021 likely remember that the prepayment rates were largely to blame for the declining dividend and earnings. As higher coupon mortgages were repaid, NLY was forced to reinvest in lower coupon mortgages, resulting in a downward trend in cash flow.

It is accurate that this was a headwind for NLY. Here is a look at NLY's MBS portfolio in December 2012: Source.

Note that the average coupon was 4.04%, and the average fair value that the book value is based on was 107.3% of par. So, you had $100.7 billion in principal, but it was valued at $108 billion.

Agency MBS repay at par 100% of the time. The only conceivable way that NLY could capture that $7+ billion was if they sold their agency MBS and didn't buy more. If they completely had exited the agency MBS business, they could have captured that premium. Otherwise, the loans carried at a fair value of $107 would eventually be repaid at $100. Since NLY's purpose of existing is to invest in agency MBS, that wasn't a practical option.

Why would anyone buy a loan that will be repaid at $100 for $107? The reason is that the coupon is higher than the coupon paid on the new MBS. You can either pay $107 to collect $4/year or $100 for $3/year. Which one is a better deal? It greatly depends on how many years the one paying $4 remains outstanding before it repays at $100. If it is one year, then you obviously prefer the $3.00 coupon. However, if the $4 coupon isn't repaid for eight years, then you will receive 8 X $4 = $32 in coupon payments and $100 when it is repaid, while the $3 coupon will only pay $24 plus $100 when it is repaid.

Hence, for much of the decade, the focus was on prepayment rates because when a debt investment is valued at a premium to par, prepayments will lower your rate of return. If you were paying attention to NLY earnings calls, they often revolved around the experienced and projected prepayment rate for this reason. The faster prepayments were, the more quickly the premium to par would dissipate and the lower yield NLY was receiving on its assets.

Some day that will be a risk for NLY again. It isn't a risk today. Here is NLY's agency MBS portfolio today: Source.

Note two things: the average coupon is 4.86%, a lot higher than in 2012. Furthermore, note that the fair value is 96.8% of par. NLY's portfolio is trading at a $2 billion discount. If a borrower repays right now, NLY gets $100 on something they are currently valuing at $96.80. They can then reinvest $100 into the current coupon MBS. So, if a prepayment happens today, NLY's book value and cash flow both go higher. Currently, if borrowers are prepaying, it is a good thing for NLY. It is only a bad thing when the MBS are trading at a significant premium to par.

So, in today's environment, worrying about prepayments is like worrying about winning the lottery because one might have to pay taxes.

It is very possible that mortgage rates will continue to decline, leading to an increase in prepayments because borrowers with higher coupon mortgages will look to refinance when rates get low enough.

What does that mean for NLY? From here, it means that we could see their MBS going up to 107% of par – or even higher, but let's stick with 107% since that was what was actually experienced in 2012. That would be approximately a $6 billion increase in the value of NLY's assets — a little under $12/share. That would be somewhat offset by their hedging portfolio, but historically those types of moves in mortgage rates have been very bullish overall for NLY's book value and share price.

Now, if you are holding NLY when mortgage rates decline, and they are sitting on massive premiums in their agency portfolio, and you want to sell because of prepayment risk, that makes a lot of sense. When their portfolio is trading discounted to par, you are essentially saying, “Don't buy this; it might go up in price!”, which is perhaps the worst reason in the world not to buy an investment.

Conclusion

When it comes to collecting high-yield income from a market that is filled with millions of dollars of dividends paid out every year, you must be aware that there are many market participants who blindly believe the myths. Many of us like to believe that if we were to time travel into the past, we could help those societies and cultures rapidly develop with the skills and knowledge that we possess. Yet, this comes with the presumption that those cultures will want to learn from you, or even be willing to allow you to teach them.

Instead of trying to convince every single myth believer that they're wrong, you can take a very different course. Allow those myths that pervade the market to enable you to find and collect big yields that others are not willing to enjoy. The beautiful thing is that they could be correct for their goals, but be entirely wrong for yours. Don't make the mistake of applying a default template to every single person's investment goals and focus. In doing so, you'll appear presumptuous, rude, and snooty. Instead, we try to recognize and encourage other investors to realize that everyone's goals are different, so how they leverage the market to achieve those goals will vary. For thousands of investors using our unique Income Method, the market is a tool that pays their way through retirement — it has been highly effective for them, but it may not be so for everyone.

When it comes to retirement, you have to have money from somewhere, whether that's an annuity, Social Security, your savings that you slowly dwindle to zero, or the market paying you recurring dividends. The source of money can vary greatly, but the need for it remains a persistent matter. For most retirees, I find that applying our unique Income Method to have the market pay your way through retirement is highly effective at addressing one of the biggest financial concerns in your life. Millions of retirees live on the brink of financial crisis because of poor planning and the adoption of methodologies that do not meet their needs.

My hope for you is that you never see financial calamity in your life, that you have a wonderful retirement, and if using our income method can help you achieve that, then I encourage you to embrace it. If your method is working for you and helping you meet your goals, then don't change. In the end, I want all of us to have the best retirement possible.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Palantir: Time To Cash Out
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NYSE:PLTR) officially joined the S&P 500 (SP500) in September this year. This finally opened the door for institutional investors, mutual funds, and analysts to purchase the stock and increase the coverage, with the stock set to benefit from higher popularity and demand.

The data analytics software company has been red-hot this year, with its value increasing 2.86x from $15/share a year ago to $43/share today.

On a year-to-date basis, Palantir's stock is up 153% alone and ranks third among the best-performing stocks in the S&P 500:

  • Vistra Corp. (VST): 225.3% YTD Return
  • NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA): 172.2% YTD Return
  • Palantir Technologies: 153.4% YTD Return.

Although this is my first coverage of the stock on Seeking Alpha, I've followed Palantir since its IPO in 2020. Even though I currently do not have any exposure to the stock, I am keeping a close eye on the company, hoping to add its shares to a portfolio on a weakness, around $20-25/share.

With Palantir's stock on fire this year and its Q3 earnings just around the corner on November 4, let's take a closer look at whether it's still a good buy heading into the earnings. Bulls generally argue Palantir's improving profitability could drive more gains in the stock price. In contrast, bears point out potential revenue deceleration, particularly as Palantir's stock trades at 29x its estimated 2025 revenue, making it one of the most expensive stocks in the S&P 500.

Palantir's Business Keeps Benefiting From AI Integration

As a first step, let's take a look at Palantir's business, given the business is relatively complex and much of its software's details are undisclosed due to the secrecy of dealing with government.

In fact, 60% of Palantir's revenue comes from government agencies, whereas the remaining 40% comes from the commercial sector, an area where the company aims to expand its operations further to drive growth.

Palantir's core business is based on three main segments:

  • Gotham: Designed for government and defense agencies, providing intelligence and data analysis tools for national security.
  • Foundry: A data integration and analytics platform that helps manage, analyze, and visualize data. Commercial clients generally use Foundry across a wide range of industries.
  • Apollo: A scalable platform for developers to build customer applications on top of Palantir's software.

Palantir generates revenue through two main avenues. Like most other software businesses, it generates revenue via licensing, charging subscription, or usage-based fees for its Gotham and Foundry platforms. In addition to licenses, the company offers consulting services, helping clients implement and optimize the software to address their custom requirements.

The business model is based on a three-phase approach: Acquire, Expand and Scale its business. In the acquisition phase, Palantir targets new customers by offering initial implementations at a very low cost or no cost at all. During the second phase, Palantir aims to hook the customer by addressing the customer's specific pain points. In the last phase, Palantir fully implements and configures solutions, allowing customers to create a symbiotic environment by adding their own software atop Palantir's platform.

Like most other software businesses, Palantir has been introducing AI into its product mix via the Artificial Intelligence Platform (“AIP”) in the last quarters, harnessing the power of large language models (“LLM”) for decision-making.

The first few used cases for Palantir are the integration of AI for its government customers, particularly aimed at intelligence gathering, counterterrorism, and military operations. However, the company is now aiming to leverage generative AI to drive growth in the commercial part of its business.

In my view, Palantir might be one of the key potential beneficiaries of generative AI. Its core strength lies in its ability to integrate and analyze large volumes of data to train models and decision-making, aligning perfectly with AI's need for high-quality, well-structured data for efficient model training.

To showcase a few successes, Palantir announced the extension of the government contract Maven Smart System for an additional five years. Palantir was an early contractor for the project launched in 2017, which aimed to bring AI to the battlefield. The estimated contract value is for it to be worth around $90M annually, or roughly 3.6% of Palantir's trailing-twelve-month revenue.

Likewise, Palantir was awarded a Department of Defense contract through the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office valued at up to $480M over the next five years and a $178M contract for project TITAN, a battlefield AI system.

From what we can see so far, Palantir's AI strategy is paying off, as it has won over major defense contracts. However, the key to unlocking future growth will be a successful execution in the more significant commercial segment.

We can see a few of such successes with customers such as Aramark, Mount Sinai, and the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency. These showcase how Palantir's AI has helped to improve supply chain management, operational efficiency, and patient care in healthcare, as presented during the AIPCon 5 back in September.

Yet, more will need to come to justify its ballooning valuation.

Commercial Segment Gaining Momentum

As we are just three weeks away from Palantir reporting its Q3 2024 earnings, let's just briefly look under the hood of Q2.

In the second-quarter earnings report, Palantir exceeded Wall Street expectations on both the top and bottom lines and issued better-than-expected Q3 guidance, with the strong performance responsible for the red-hot year-to-date price performance.

For Q2, Palantir reported GAAP earnings of $0.09/share, roughly an 80% YoY increase. Revenue rose 27% to $678M, beating analysts' estimates of $0.08 per share and $653M in revenue.

Both the government and commercial businesses performed very well during the quarter. The government business generated $371M in revenue, up 23% from the analyst's estimates of $349M. Its commercial business has delivered even better performance, with 33% revenue growth and revenue at $307M, ahead of the forecast by $1M.

Commercial revenue was significantly helped by a substantial 55% growth in US Commercial revenue, which reached $159M.

Palantir's future depends on its success in growing its commercial segment, which is gaining momentum as the company strengthens its expansion into financial, healthcare, and energy, moving away from a government-dependent revenue structure.

Even though government-based contracts are more guaranteed and less cyclical, most of the money is to be made in the commercial segment.

In fact, CEO Alex Karp, in a letter to shareholders, highlighted that Palantir's US commercial customer base increased 83% year over year, from 161 customers in Q2 2023 to 295 as of Q2 2024, now making up half of the company's total customer base.

Top & Bottom-Line Growth Do Not Disappoint

Looking forward, Palantir appears to be on a solid footing to continue delivering, with the firm's billing and remaining performance obligations (“RPO”) increasing 19% and 42% YoY, respectively.

These two metrics serve for us as evidence of the strong demand for the underlying Palantir's software solutions. This is particularly as Palantir is facing higher demand than it can immediately serve, with customers very likely to stick around given Palantir's software's high switching costs.

Thanks to the strong demand, Palantir's management has raised its outlook for FY24 with estimated 2024 sales of $2.75B and $970M in operating income, an increase from the previous expectations of $2.68B of revenue and $874M of operating income.

Compared to full-FY23 revenue of $2.23B, this would represent a revenue growth of 23.3% with operating income expected to expand as much as 8x.

Nonetheless, given the strong tailwinds the business is experiencing, particularly in the US commercial segment, I expect the company to beat even its revised guidance by the end of this year. I further expect another 20-23% annual revenue growth on tap in the subsequent years.

Even as many growth companies prioritize revenue growth, leaving profitability in the back seat or straight out failing to convert revenue into earnings, that's undoubtedly not Palantir's case.

From what we can see below, Palantir's business became profitable on the operating level only in 2023, but operating income has continuously expanded, driven by higher revenue and margins.

Palantir's operating margins have been continuously improving. Thanks to the attractive unit economics the business can generate from the existing user base by upselling new products, I see margin expansion as the likely path for the company as it grows.

Valuation Might Act As An Anchor On Future Returns

The issue with Palantir's business is not the business itself, but the valuation—an issue we face as investors with most other high-quality companies.

Let's first take a look at Palantir's Price-to-Sales valuation:

  • Palantir's management expects the FY24 sales to land at $2.75B, which would imply a P/S ratio of 35x, given the $97B market cap.
  • Analysts are expecting forward FY25 sales to land at $3.32B, a YoY growth of 22%, implying a 14-month forward P/S valuation of 29x.

Now, the rich valuation, even for large-cap stock such as Palantir, would be justifiable if the company exhibited massive EPS expansion over the coming years.

As per FactSet, analysts are forecasting Palantir's EPS to grow at the following rate:

  • FY24 EPS: $0.35E, YoY growth: 42%
  • FY25 EPS: $0.42E, YoY growth: 19%
  • FY26 EPS: $0.51E, YoY growth: 21%.

The growth we see above indeed categorizes Palantir's stock as a genuine growth company. Still, the 20% EPS growth expectations beyond FY24 are, from my perspective, not enough to justify the exuberant valuation the stock carries.

Let's look at the following scenarios. How much does Palantir's bottom-line need to expand for the remainder of the decade to justify buying the shares at today's prices?

In Scenario 1, if we assume the projected FY24, FY25, and FY26 growth materializes as expected by the analysts and the company grows its EPS at 30% annually for the remainder of the decade, Palantir would need to trade at least at 40x its P/E by the end of 2029 to just break-even on your money or negative real return, if adjusted per inflation.

Given the non-existent margin of safety and uncertain future growth, I would aim for at least 20% ROR to justify buying Palantir's shares at today's valuation. If we stick to the same growth assumptions as above, Palantir's shares would need to trade at 100x its earnings by the end of 2029 if I required the stock to deliver at 20% annualized ROR.

I am not saying Scenario 2 is impossible to materialize, but hoping for a 100x P/E valuation in 5.5 years is inherently uncertain and risky, and I do not like to bet allocate my heard-earned cash into uncertain promises.

Naturally, it's possible that Palantir will beat expectations and grow its bottom line much quicker than expected. In Scenario 3, I am assuming 40% EPS growth between today and the end of 2029, a truly hyper-scaler if this growth materializes. However, even in that case, we would need to see the stock trading at around 50x its earnings if we aimed to achieve at least 16% annualized ROR.

This indicates that it is critical to consider the perspective from which we examine Palantir's growth and valuation before making an investment decision.

Forecasting Palantir's future growth is inherently uncertain, given its relatively short life as a public company.

That's perhaps one reason why analysts' opinions on a short-term price target differ widely. However, the average price target is $27.7/share, implying a more than 30% downside from today's share price.

Investor's Takeaway

I believe Palantir is well-positioned for long-term success in the software industry, particularly in establishing itself as a leader in AI platforms. Its access to enterprise data is an excellent start for efficient AI data model learning, eventually building a competitive advantage.

The company is helping government and commercial companies worldwide convert data into decisions, and Palantir is set to materially benefit from organizations' thirst for data-driven decision-making.

I also expect Palantir's consumer footprint to materially increase over the coming years while the business benefits from unit economics, upselling its new product offerings to existing customers and improving profitability.

Nevertheless, investors should be cautious about Palantir's valuation. The sky-high expectations are exposing investors to significant risks of permanent capital loss if the growth does not pan out as expected.

data.title: U.S. REITs Log Dip In Short Interest In September
data.description:
Short interest in US equity real estate investment trusts ticked down on average during September.

Average REIT short interest fell 8 basis points during the month to 3.7% of shares outstanding, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

'Other retail' REITs log largest decline in short interest

The "other retail" sector, which includes single-tenant retail and outlet center REITs, recorded the largest drop in average short interest in September, down 79 basis points to 4.9% of shares outstanding.

Single-tenant retail-focused Netstreit Corp. (NTST) posted the biggest decline in short interest as a percentage of shares outstanding of any US REIT during the month, down 5.4 percentage points to 7.3%. Netstreit closed the month as the 18th most-shorted US REIT versus being the seventh most-shorted REIT at the end of August.

Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) and Agree Realty Corp. (ADC) were also among the REITs with the largest declines in short interest relative to their shares outstanding for September. Short interest in Getty Realty fell 2.8 percentage points to 4.1% of its shares outstanding, while short interest in Agree Realty dropped 1.5 percentage points to 8.8% of shares outstanding.

The hotel sector followed, with a 60-basis point drop in average short interest as a percentage of shares outstanding. Hotels closed the third quarter as the third-most-shorted REIT property sector, trailing the healthcare and office sectors.

Within the hotel sector, short interest in Sunstone Hotel Investors Inc. (SHO) as a percentage of shares outstanding fall 2.3 percentage points to 5.2%, while RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) recorded a 1.7-percentage-point decline in short interest to 7.0% of shares outstanding.

The office sector remained the most-shorted REIT property sector, at 5.3% of shares outstanding on average.

The advertising REIT sector booked the biggest increase in average short interest as a percentage of shares outstanding, up 1.4 percentage points to 4.0% of shares outstanding.

Advertising REIT OUTFRONT Media Inc. (OUT) registered the third-largest increase in short interest, up 2.9 percentage points to 5.9% of its shares outstanding.

REITs with the most change in short interest

Among all US REITs, shopping center-focused Wheeler Real Estate Investment Trust Inc. (WHLR) logged the largest increase in short interest in September, up 16 percentage points to 32.4% of shares outstanding.

Multifamily REIT Independence Realty Trust Inc. (IRT) ranked second with a 4.4-percentage-point increase in short interest to 10.0% of shares outstanding.

Conversely, Netstreit posted the largest decline in short interest, followed by multifamily REIT Centerspace, which logged a 4.3-percentage-point decline in short interest to 0.9% of its shares outstanding.

The third-biggest decline was logged by healthcare REIT Medical Properties Trust Inc. (MPW), with short interest down 3.6 percentage points to 35.9% of shares outstanding.

REITs with the largest short positions

Medical Properties Trust remained the most-shorted US REIT stock, with 215.2 million shares sold short.

Wheeler REIT ranked second, with 202,282 shares sold short.

Office REIT Hudson Pacific Properties Inc. (HPP) rounded out the top three, with short interest equal to 16.4% of shares outstanding.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Build-A-Bear: Dividend Stock Has Potential To Become A Long-Term Powerhouse
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Dividend Collectuh

Stumbling across Build-A-Bear Workshop (NYSE:BBW), a company I'm very familiar with, I didn't for once think the stock was one who paid a dividend. Honestly, I didn't even think they were a public company until recently.

But looking through the company's financials, I was utterly impressed as they seemed to have the makings to become a great, long-term dividend stock. Yes, BBW pays a dividend!

Despite being public since 2004, the specialty retailer only recently started paying a consistent dividend. In this article, I discuss the company's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why they have the potential to become a great, long-term dividend stock worth holding.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, the company has been public since 2004. They were founded in 1997 and has become very popular throughout the years, mainly amongst children. Currently, they operate in three segments and have store locations globally, which I'll discuss more later in the article.

The majority of their stores, however, are located in the U.S. & Canada. The company is exactly as it sounds, offering customers the option to build, and create their own stuffed animals that you can customize with various clothing and miscellaneous items.

Latest Earnings

Despite higher for longer interest rates, which have plagued consumers, Build-A-Bear has performed solidly, albeit facing headwinds. As a discretionary retailer, it's no surprise as consumers have felt the effects of interest rates, more so in the past year.

However, there seems to be light at the end of the tunnel with the 50 basis points rate cut this past September, and this should provide tailwinds for retailers like BBW.

Build-A-Bear reported their Q2 earnings at the end of August, with EPS of $0.64 and revenue of $111.8 million. While EPS saw a significant decline from $1.57 year-over-year, revenue increased from $109.2 million.

Net retail sales were flat at $103.5 million, something I assume could also be attributed to cost conscious consumers feeling the effects of high interest rates. Management attributed this to the timing of product launches in-store and online, leading to sales increasing 8% in the prior year's quarter.

However, the company has remained resilient and continued to perform well. Gross profit margins continued expanding, increasing 50 basis points from the previous year to 54.2%. These were flat on a sequential basis.

Commercial revenue, which takes into account international revenue as well as wholesale sales to partner operators, was up 44.8% vs 2023, largely due to commercial margin expansion.

Continued Expansion

One metric I was impressed with is the company's continued expansion, especially into international markets. I really thought BBW was something niche located inside shopping malls.

During the quarter, the company continued to expand, opening an additional 17 stores, including franchise locations. Some notable stores included two in Las Vegas, which I thought was very smart and strategic on the company's part.

As a frequent visitor, Las Vegas is one of the most visited tourist destinations in the country, and I suspect they will see increased foot traffic over the coming months/years due to pent-up demand. This will likely benefit BBW as they are likely to see an increase in foot traffic as well.

The city is still growing with Formula 1 racing and various sporting events such as the new 30,000 seat baseball stadium replacing the Tropicana. BBW opened locations at the Welcome to Las Vegas Gift Shops on the Las Vegas Strip.

Although Las Vegas is known for its adult activities, the city is no stranger to children and traveling families. Adults may want to visit the store to buy or bring back gifts for their children at home. What better gift than a stuffed animal from Build-A-Bear, right?

Outside of the U.S., BBW continued its expansion into Italy and France as well as South America, South Africa, and China. The company is on track to open 50 new locations this fiscal year, an increase from 37 in 2023. They are also currently in over 20 countries, and I expect them to continue expanding at a rapid pace over the coming years.

Debt-Free Balance Sheet

One reason I think BBW can become a great, long-term dividend stocks is the flexibility the company has being debt-free. Aside from no debt, Build-A-Bear's cash position was also strong with $25.2 million. This gives the retailer ample room and flexibility to continue their expansion efforts as well as grow the dividend, which I'll delve into shortly.

Moreover, with interest rates still remaining high, and uncertainty surrounding them due to a recent stronger than anticipated jobs report, BBW is in a comfortable position whether rates remain higher for longer or not.

Dividend

As previously mentioned, I wouldn't expect BBW to pay a dividend, let alone a respectable one with a forward yield of 2.24%. The company has also paid out two special dividends and frequently repurchased its shares over the past few quarters.

During the 1H of 2024, management has returned significant capital to its shareholders, totaling $24 million through buybacks & dividends. In 2023, they returned $42.4 million to shareholders, which prompted them to initiate a $0.20 quarterly dividend going forward.

This shows the company's confidence in their ability to generate strong cash flows going forward, which I suspect they will do through partnerships and continued expansion.

In the chart below, you can see how the company's free cash flow has grown exponentially over the past 3 years. Additionally, their balance sheet allows them the ability to continue repurchasing shares, which ultimately should lead to earnings and share price growth.

Undervalued?

With a forward P/E of just 9.18x, Build-A-Bear appears to also be undervalued. Using their earnings estimate of $3.72 for the fiscal year, this represents a growth rate of just 1.92% from $3.65 brought in 2023. But, looking out beyond 2026, earnings are expected to grow at an average rate of 10.17%. And this should lead to solid price appreciation in the coming years.

For comparison purposes, BBW's forward P/E is significantly lower than the sector average of 16.94x and MarineMax's (HZO) 11.95x, further signaling the stock could be undervalued. Additionally, BBW's P/E is below their 5-year average of 10.64x.

As seen by the company's strategic partnerships, store openings, and free cash flow generation, I anticipate the company warranting a higher valuation in the coming years. Wall Street currently rates them a strong buy with a price target of roughly $40, upside of 16%.

Using their earnings growth rate average of 7.28% and 4% to manage expectations and account for slower growth after, I have a long-term price target of $62.23, more than 82% potential upside! Moreover, seeing the company's growth and execution, I think this is attainable due to their rapid expansion, buybacks, and debt-free balance sheet.

Risks & Bottom Line

But, due to the company's business model, they may not be an ideal portfolio holding, especially for long-term dividend investors. Additionally, their current yield may not be as appealing to some. As a consumer discretionary retailer that sells stuffed animals, it's likely the company can see flat or declining sales in the coming months/years.

And while their fundamentals remain solid, this could lead to a decline in share price. Furthermore, a slowdown in the economy would also likely impact the company's sales as job losses tick up, giving consumers less capital to spend on discretionary items.

However, with interest rates already cut by 50 basis points, and more expected in the future, this should provide tailwinds for companies like Build-A-Bear, thus positively impacting their net sales.

And as they continue to grow cash flow and maintain a debt-free balance sheet, this should aid in continued expansion, ultimately leading to share price appreciation over the long term. Due to this, along with their low, single-digit P/E, I currently assign Build-A-Bear a buy rating.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast: Surprise Jitters
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Surprise jitters

More than $420 billion. That's how much value Bloomberg estimates chip stocks globally have shed after their Dutch peer ASML Holdings (ASML) lowered its forecasts for 2025 and revealed that it booked about half the orders Wall Street expected in the third quarter. While ASML's shares tanked about 17%, the news also took down other semiconductor stocks: Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Applied Materials (AMAT), and Lam Research (LRCX).

Zoom in: On Tuesday, chip equipment supplier ASML slashed its net sales forecast to €30B - €35B ($32.7B - $38.1B) from previous expectations of €30B to €40B. The guidance came in about 15% to 20% below expectations. For Q3, ASML's net orders were €2.6B compared to the consensus of €5.39B. Meanwhile, quarterly net sales were €7.47B, beating estimates of €7.17B. CEO Christophe Fouquet mentioned that while development in artificial intelligence remains robust, other market segments like logic and memory were taking longer to recover, and this trend would likely continue into 2025.

Export curbs at play? For ASML, a larger worry is the business they are losing in China due to current U.S. and Dutch export restrictions set in place to curb China's AI capabilities. "So we expect China to come in at around 20% of our total revenue for next year. Which would also be in line with its representation in our backlog," ASML CFO Roger Dassen said. This means a massive reduction, as revenue from China accounted for 49% of ASML's revenue in the previous quarter. Separately, a recent Bloomberg report mentioned that U.S. officials have also been in talks to set a cap on export licenses for certain nations in the interest of national security.

SA commentary: "With a primary growth driver, China, gone, it could be a while before investor sentiment on ASML Holding N.V. stock turns around. I don't think shareholders need to sell, but we could be range-bound for the foreseeable future," said Kumquat Research in ASML: Export Restrictions Kill Key Growth Driver. Expecting ASML's revenue growth to be driven by high numerical aperture EUV products, Hunter Wolf Research said, "I think the headwinds are fully priced in their current stock price. I am buying the dip today."

What else is happening...

Meta (META) must face lawsuits over profiting from youth social media addiction.

United Airlines (UAL) beats Q3 estimates as it sheds 'unprofitable capacity'.

Qualcomm (QCOM) to wait until after US election to make Intel decision.

Fund managers move away from defensive sectors and rotate into cyclicals.

Google (GOOGL) Cloud says Nvidia Blackwell line 'running in our labs'.

LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) trades lower after China weakness drags down results.

Consumers see higher probability of missing debt payments in next 3 months.

Tesla (TSLA) lands key approval in Germany as it looks to ramp up production.

Bitcoin hits 2-month high above $67k after ‘monster’ inflows into spot ETFs.

Harris or Trump? Shouldn’t matter for markets – Oppenheimer.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -1.8%. Hong Kong -0.2%. China +0.1%. India -0.4%.

In Europe, at midday, London +0.6%. Paris -0.6%. Frankfurt -0.6%.

Futures at 6:15, Dow -0.1%. S&P flat. Nasdaq +0.1%. Crude -0.2% at $70.43. Gold +0.7% to $2,696.50. Bitcoin +2.9% to $67,608.

Ten-year Treasury Yield flat at 4.01%.

Today's Economic Calendar

07:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications
08:30 AM Import/Export Prices
11:00 AM Treasury Buyback Announcement
2:00 PM Treasury Buyback Results

Companies reporting earnings today »

data.title: Grid Dynamics Is Well-Positioned To Deliver AI Transformations
data.description:
Grid Dynamics (NASDAQ:GDYN) is one of the lesser-known but highly impactful digital transformation companies I have come across. It serves Fortune 500 companies, among others, and is well-positioned amid the current high-growth trends in AI demand. Based on my valuation analysis and expectations for the company's growth in FY25, I believe it could achieve a market cap expansion of 10% in 12 months. In addition, Q3 2024 should offer a good validation for the recent three-month price return of over 40%.

Operational Analysis

Grid Dynamics is a global digital engineering and IT services provider that focuses on enabling enterprise-scale companies to drive growth, efficiency, and impact. Its four core services involve technology consulting, platform and product engineering, AI and advanced analytics, and cloud and DevOps. It serves a wide range of industry verticals, including retail, high-tech, pharma, life sciences, and wealth management, among others.

There are multiple growth catalysts worth outlining that support the long-term investment case for Grid Dynamics. Firstly, it has significantly expanded its AI capabilities and now offers around 30 solutions, targeting Fortune 500 companies. Additionally, management has partnered with Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) Cloud to implement generative AI solutions, which is expected to drive further growth. As an example, their AI-driven Data Observability Starter Kit improves data quality across platforms, allowing companies to make more informed decisions.

'Grid Dynamics Recognized as a Leader in Everest Group’s Inaugural Google Cloud Services Specialists PEAK Matrix® Assessments 2024' — Grid Dynamics

In addition, Grid Dynamics has a robust acquisition strategy, including recent purchases of Mobile Computing and JUXT. The former expands its footprint in the Americas, particularly in manufacturing, consumer packaged goods, and financial services, and the latter strengthens its position in the banking and financial services industries, especially within the European market, reinforcing its position as a leader in complex data solutions.

I expect the company to have a strong future ahead of it as digital transformations are likely to become more prominent and sophisticated. I see it as highly likely that demand for technology consultants and facilitators will increase exponentially over time. The company also has a global delivery model, and its follow-the-sun (24/7) approach ensures continuous service availability across time zones. This allows it to have a much more efficient international growth trajectory.

Despite the strong growth horizon, technology consulting and IT services are saturated fields. Accenture (ACN), Cognizant (CTSH), and Infosys (INFY) are some core examples that pose a significant threat to Grid Dynamics' long-term growth. Grid Dynamics is the smallest of the companies in this cohort, and so I do expect there to be significant inhibitions in its long-term expansion due to the market consolidation already achieved by Accenture. This is why the company's tailored focus on areas like AI and digital transformation is important, as too broad an operational focus undoubtedly makes it prone to being outcompeted by larger competitors with more resources.

Financial & Valuation Analysis

Grid Dynamics had its Q2 results at the beginning of August, where it achieved a 7.4% year-over-year growth in its normalized EPS. From reading the earnings presentation, the company's wide (and growing) diversification stood out to me not only based on its industry exposure but also its customer concentration. This signifies that the business is becoming less risky, with a greater pool of revenue streams to protect it in the event of recessions or other catalysts contributing to the loss of customers.

Looking ahead to Q3, which is expected on 10/31, the company has guided for revenue of approximately $85 million (9.8% year-over-year growth) and non-GAAP EBITDA of approximately $12.8 million. The current consensus is that the company will achieve year-over-year normalized EPS growth of 12%. In Q2, the Retail and Technology, Media, and Telecom ('TMT') sectors have been significant contributors to its revenue, a trend sure to continue into Q3. The quarter will likely be marked by good growth due to the heavy digital emphasis around the world at the moment, particularly with the advent of AI—a trend I expect will likely compound in 2025. Therefore, it is not surprising to me that the stock is up 12.6% year-to-date and 43.4% in the past three months.

However, given the recent momentum, the stock's valuation is not as attractive as it was three months ago. For example, the company's TTM EV-to-sales ratio is a 27.5% contraction from its five-year average, but its forward revenue growth is a much more stark 73% contraction from its five-year average. This signifies that the stock is potentially overvalued. However, compared to its peers, it has the second lowest EV-to-sales ratio, with only Cognizant coming in lower.

While the companies I have chosen have a much broader operational focus than Grid Dynamics, they are also among the best technology consulting and digital transformation firms I know of. As such, my peer comparison offers investors an assessment of relative merit for portfolio allocation.

Furthermore, it is worth noting that its TTM price-to-operating-cash-flow ratio is currently a 52.3% contraction from its five-year average, and its forward operating cash flow is an almost equal 55.3% contraction from its five-year average. In conjunction with its EV-to-sales ratio compared to its peers, this suggests that the stock could be deemed fairly valued at this time.

I believe 2025 will be a strong year for Grid Dynamics, driven by lower interest rates boosting demand for digital transformation, as well as continued robust demand for enterprise AI development trends that I expect to persist for the next year or two before tapering. Therefore, I estimate the company will achieve revenue growth of 15% for the full-year Fiscal 2025, ending December 2025. With my total revenue estimate of $385.25 million for December 2025, if the company trades at a P/S ratio of 3.25 (a slight contraction from present levels), its market cap will be $1.25 billion. This is a 9.65% growth from the current market cap of $1.14 billion.

However, comparing Grid Dynamics to more direct competitors of a similar market cap, such as CI&T (CINT) and The Hackett Group (HCKT), shows some weakness in its valuation. It's important to note that Grid Dynamics is currently reporting a net loss, and while this is a valid reason to be cautious about the size of an allocation, I consider the company best evaluated at this time based on its revenues. Given this, one could ascertain that Grid Dynamics carries considerably more risk than its peers, CI&T and The Hackett Group. However, when compared to larger organizations like Accenture, Cognizant, and Infosys, I believe the stock is fairly valued or moderately overvalued, particularly if one is uncertain about its future profit-generation capabilities. That said, the company has already proven it can generate a net profit in 2023, so I believe it's only a matter of time before this stabilizes.

Risk Analysis

While I mentioned the positives in Grid Dynamics' customer and industry diversification in my Q2 results analysis, there is one caveat to this. The company generates 76.1% of its operating revenue from the United States as of the 2023 10-K. Therefore, it is more prone to downside volatility in the case of a significant macroeconomic recession in the West, which I believe we could face soon. This is further reinforced by the fact that the company derives the majority of the remainder of its operating revenue from the United Kingdom and the Netherlands. While 2025's lower interest rates are likely to provide significant tailwinds for Grid Dynamics (reinforcing my 12-month market cap target) in the medium term, I think high inflation and lower GDP growth rates in the U.S. and other Western allies could cause growth inhibitions for smaller companies operating in the region. Aligning with my broader long-term outlook right now, making portfolios geographically multipolar is a priority.

Furthermore, I believe that the future of digital transformation will become more cyclical. Similar to the demand rush for semiconductors during periods of technological advancements, Grid Dynamics is likely to face periods of high demand for digital transformation related to broader trends in AI and robotics and periods where budgets have been drained temporarily. This should be kept in mind for long-term investors, but the good news is, I believe we are at the very early stages of the next major upcycle related to AI.

Conclusion

I do believe this is a viable long-term holding. However, investors should expect moderate near-term results, with the greatest benefits likely to be accrued over many years if the company continues to establish itself in niche areas of digital transformation related to AI. The company shows some signs of overvaluation, so I do expect a near-term contraction in its P/S ratio. Nonetheless, even given this, I find a 10% growth in its market cap likely over the next 12 months.
